titleZh: 酒店入住率上来了，老板今天先查哪 5 个利润漏点？
titleEn: Occupancy Is Up. Where Should Hotel Owners Look for the First Five Profit Leaks?
summaryZh: 房间卖出去了，前台忙起来了，月底一算账——利润没怎么涨。
summaryEn: Rooms are selling. The front desk is busy. End of month — the profit numbers don't match the revenue.
contentZh:
酒店入住率上来了，老板今天先查哪 5 个利润漏点？

房间卖出去了，前台忙起来了，月底一算账——利润没怎么涨。

这不是错觉。入住率是面子，利润是里子。很多时候房间卖出去了，利润却在你看不到的地方一点点漏掉了。今天不查大账，先查最容易漏的五个地方。

1. 渠道佣金与房价结构

很多人只看OTA平台卖了多少间夜，很少算每间房实际到账多少钱。携程、美团、飞猪、抖音——每个平台的佣金比例不一样，每个渠道的促销补贴也不一样。旺季的时候，平台流量大，但促销力度也大。可能有30%的房间是通过高佣金渠道卖掉的，到了月底才发现，一算净收入，比淡季还低。

今天就能查的事：打开你的PMS或者渠道对账单，看看最近两周每个渠道的实际净收入占比。哪个渠道佣金最高但房量最低？哪个渠道佣金低但被压价最狠？

2. 人工排班与加班成本

入住率上来了，人不够用，于是加人、加班、加临时工。但很多人不知道，一家酒店从70%入住率拉到90%，人工成本可能涨20%到30%——不是按比例涨的，是跳涨的。

因为90%入住率意味着几乎满房状态：退房集中、做房密集、早餐翻台压力翻倍、夜班需要多留人。多出的每班次都不便宜。

今天就能查的事：对比最近四周的人房比（员工数÷可售间夜数）和人均加班小时。如果人房比没变但加班费翻倍了，说明排班结构有问题。

3. 早餐、布草、能耗等变动成本

入住率高的时候，变动成本上涨最快的是三个地方：早餐食材和耗材、布草洗涤周转、水电能耗。

早餐成本好算，人均成本乘以入住人数。布草洗涤是按条算的，满房时换洗量翻倍，烘干和熨烫能耗也翻倍。能耗方面，夏季空调全开、热水供应量增大——很多酒店满房月电费是淡季的两倍以上。

今天就能查的事：翻一下厨房采购清单、洗涤对账单、上个月电费单。对比去年同期，有没有毛利率下降但单价没变的品类。

4. 取消、退款与差评补偿

入住率高的月份，取消率和退款率也在上升。满房时取消的位置很难再卖出去，尤其是当天取消——卖不掉的房间就是纯亏损。差评补偿也在增加：赠送早餐券、升级房型、送水果、折扣下次入住——这些都是账面成本，但很少有人汇总算这笔账。

今天就能查的事：在PMS里拉一份差评补偿清单，按房间号、房费折扣、赠品价值算总价。然后除以当月可售间夜收入，看看差评补偿占了多少。

5. 复购和直客沉淀不足

高入住率最大的隐形成本，是销售团队在旺季忙着接单，没时间做复购和直客沉淀。旺季过后，客人的联系方式还是停留在OTA的订单里，酒店自己没有留下任何触达渠道。

损失的不是一个订单，而是下一次客人出游时选择你的几率。

今天就能查的事：看看过去三个月，公众号粉丝增长了多少，企业微信加了几个客人，短信或者微信触达了几次。如果这些数字是零，旺季再忙也要开始填这个缺口。

酒店入住率高为什么还是不赚钱？

因为入住率只是数量指标，不是效益指标。渠道结构、排班效率、变动成本、隐形损失和客户资产沉淀，这五个环节有一个漏了，利润就会被吃掉。

酒店利润漏点通常在哪里？

最集中的五个地方就是上面说的渠道佣金结构、人工排班效率、变动成本管理、差评补偿损耗、客户资产沉淀。大部分酒店的利润不是在竞争中被吃掉的，而是在日常运营中慢慢漏掉的。

酒店老板每天应该看哪些经营数据？

如果只能看五个数，建议看：当日可售间夜收入、各渠道实际净收入、人房比、取消率加补偿成本占比、直客预订占比。这几个数字连起来看，比只看入住率和平均房价更能说明问题。

迈创兄弟C&T（MarvelBros C&T）可以做系统的经营诊断和成本控制。如果你不确定自己酒店的利润漏在哪里，可以先从今天这五个方向查一遍——大概率能找到一到两个已经漏了很久的口子。
contentEn:
Occupancy Is Up. Where Should Hotel Owners Look for the First Five Profit Leaks?

Rooms are selling. The front desk is busy. End of month — the profit numbers don't match the revenue.

This is not an illusion. Occupancy is the headline. Profit is the bottom line. And a lot of the time, rooms are going out the door while money is quietly leaking somewhere you are not looking. Skip the big accounts for now. Start with the five places it leaks most.

1. Channel Commission and Rate Structure

Most hotel owners look at how many room nights each OTA sold. Few calculate what actually hits the bank account per room. Ctrip, Meituan, Fliggy, Douyin — each platform takes a different commission rate, and each runs different promotional discounts during peak season. In high season, platform traffic surges, but so do promotion costs. It is not unusual to find that 30 percent of your rooms went through the highest-commission channels, and by the end of the month, your net revenue is lower than it was in the off-season.

What to check today: Pull your PMS or channel settlement report. Look at the net revenue share per channel for the last two weeks. Which channel charges the highest commission but contributes the lowest volume? Which channel has low commission but is squeezing you hardest on rate?

2. Staff Scheduling and Overtime Costs

When occupancy goes up, the instinct is to add people — more shifts, more overtime, more temporary hires. But here is what many miss: moving from 70 percent to 90 percent occupancy can increase labor cost by 20 to 30 percent. It is not linear. It jumps.

At 90 percent, the hotel is effectively full. Checkouts are concentrated, housekeeping is compressed, breakfast service pressure doubles, and night shifts need extra bodies. Every extra shift costs more than the last one.

What to check today: Compare the last four weeks of staff-to-room ratio (total employees divided by available room nights) and average overtime hours per employee. If the ratio stayed flat but overtime doubled, the scheduling structure is the problem.

3. Variable Costs: Breakfast, Linen, and Energy

Three variable cost items spike fastest during high occupancy: breakfast ingredients and disposables, linen washing cycles, and energy consumption.

Breakfast cost is straightforward — per-person cost multiplied by guest count. Linen is charged per piece, and at full occupancy, wash volume doubles, and so do drying and ironing energy costs. On energy, summer air conditioning runs full tilt, and hot water demand surges. Many hotels find their full-occupancy electricity bill is more than double the off-season figure.

What to check today: Pull the kitchen purchasing report, the laundry invoice, and last month's utility bill. Compare against the same period last year. Identify categories where gross margin dropped but unit cost stayed flat.

4. Cancellations, Refunds, and Review Compensation

Higher occupancy months also see higher cancellation and refund rates. A room cancelled on the same day during a full house is nearly impossible to resell — that is pure loss. Compensation for bad reviews is also rising: complimentary breakfast vouchers, room upgrades, fruit baskets, next-stay discounts. These are real costs on the books, but few people total them up.

What to check today: Run a review compensation report from your PMS. Total it by room number, discount value, and gift cost. Divide it by total sellable room revenue for the month. See what percentage of revenue is being given back.

5. Repeat Bookings and Direct Guest Retention

The hidden cost of high occupancy is that the sales team is too busy processing orders to work on repeat business and direct guest acquisition. After the season ends, guest contact data stays inside the OTA order history. The hotel has no direct channel to reach them.

What is lost is not one booking. It is the probability that guest chooses you again next time.

What to check today: Look at the last three months. How many new followers on your WeChat account? How many guests were added to your enterprise WeChat? How many SMS or WeChat touch-points were sent? If the numbers are zero, start filling the gap — even during peak season.

Why Isn't Profit Growing Even When Occupancy Is Up?

Because occupancy is a volume metric, not a profit metric. Channel structure, scheduling efficiency, variable cost control, hidden loss from compensation, and guest asset retention — if any one of these five areas is leaking, the profit gets eaten.

Where Do Hotel Profit Leaks Usually Appear?

The five most concentrated areas are the ones above: channel commission and rate structure, staff scheduling efficiency, variable cost management, review compensation and refund loss, and repeat guest retention. Most hotels do not lose profit to competition. They lose it to slow leaks in daily operations.

What Five Numbers Should Hotel Owners Look at Every Day?

If you can only look at five numbers, start with: daily sellable room revenue, net revenue per channel, staff-to-room ratio, cancellation rate plus compensation cost share, and direct booking share. Tracked together, these five tell you more about your real financial health than occupancy and ADR alone.

MarvelBros C&T offers operational diagnostics and cost control analysis. Start with the five checks above. There is a good chance you will find at least one leak that has been running for a while.
author: 迈创兄弟C&T（MarvelBros C&T）
